title: Mechanical Small Bowel Obstruction in a 64-Year-Old with Prior Abdominal Surgery (Adhesions): Recognizing the Classic Picture, Correcting Fluid/Electrolyte Loss, NPO + NASOGASTRIC DECOMPRESSION, Vigilant Monitoring for STRANGULATION/Perforation, and Coordinating Conservative-vs-Surgical Management
subtitle: A SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION (SBO) is a MECHANICAL blockage of the forward flow of intestinal contents. The MOST COMMON cause is post-surgical ADHESIONS (scar bands from prior abdominal/pelvic surgery); other causes include incarcerated HERNIAS, TUMORS, VOLVULUS, intussusception, strictures (e.g., Crohn disease), and gallstone ileus. This is distinct from a PARALYTIC ILEUS — a functional (non-mechanical) failure of peristalsis from recent surgery, opioids, electrolyte derangements (hypokalemia), peritonitis, or immobility — which presents with a silent, distended abdomen and absent bowel sounds rather than the mechanical pattern below. CLASSIC PRESENTATION of mechanical SBO: CRAMPY, COLICKY, intermittent abdominal PAIN; abdominal DISTENSION; VOMITING (early and profuse, often bilious, with a PROXIMAL obstruction; later, more distended and FECULENT/feculent-smelling with a DISTAL obstruction); and OBSTIPATION (inability to pass stool OR flatus, especially with a complete obstruction). BOWEL SOUNDS are classically HIGH-PITCHED, TINKLING, and hyperactive (borborygmi, rushes) EARLY/above the obstruction, then become HYPOACTIVE/ABSENT as the bowel fatigues or ischemia develops. FLUID & ELECTROLYTE consequences are central: vomiting plus 'THIRD-SPACING' of fluid into the obstructed, edematous bowel lumen/wall cause HYPOVOLEMIA (tachycardia, hypotension, low urine output, elevated BUN), and loss of gastric/intestinal contents produces HYPOKALEMIA and (with proximal/gastric loss) a metabolic ALKALOSIS — these derangements are an immediate physiologic threat. The CRITICAL distinction is SIMPLE vs STRANGULATED/closed-loop obstruction: a STRANGULATED obstruction has COMPROMISED BLOOD SUPPLY to the bowel, leading to ISCHEMIA → NECROSIS → PERFORATION → peritonitis and sepsis, and is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY. RED FLAGS for strangulation/ischemia/perforation: pain that becomes SEVERE and CONSTANT (rather than colicky), FEVER, TACHYCARDIA, hypotension, PERITONEAL SIGNS (rigidity, guarding, rebound tenderness), a rising LACTATE and WBC, and metabolic acidosis. DIAGNOSIS: abdominal imaging — upright/flat abdominal X-RAY shows dilated bowel loops with AIR-FLUID LEVELS and a 'string-of-pearls' sign, while FREE AIR under the diaphragm indicates PERFORATION; CT scan localizes the obstruction, identifies the cause and the transition point, and detects ischemia/closed-loop obstruction. MANAGEMENT: initial care is supportive and the SAME first steps for nearly every SBO — make the patient NPO; insert a NASOGASTRIC (NG) TUBE to LOW INTERMITTENT SUCTION to DECOMPRESS the stomach/proximal bowel (relieves distension and vomiting, rests the bowel, and reduces aspiration risk); give IV ISOTONIC FLUID resuscitation and CORRECT ELECTROLYTES (replace potassium, address alkalosis); maintain STRICT INTAKE & OUTPUT (including NG output) and monitor labs and perfusion; provide analgesia and antiemetics; and perform SERIAL abdominal assessments and vital signs to detect deterioration. DEFINITIVE management depends on the type: a PARTIAL or adhesive obstruction often RESOLVES with this CONSERVATIVE management (bowel rest + decompression + resuscitation) over hours to days; a COMPLETE, CLOSED-LOOP, or STRANGULATED obstruction (or one that fails to resolve or shows signs of ischemia) requires SURGERY (lysis of adhesions, hernia repair, resection of necrotic bowel). KEY 'DO-NOTs': do NOT give oral feeding or bulk-forming fiber/oral laxatives to 'push it through' (worsens distension/vomiting/aspiration), and do NOT give ANTIMOTILITY agents (e.g., loperamide) for the vomiting (they worsen the obstruction and risk perforation/toxic dilation). NURSING ROLE: recognize the SBO picture and its cause; restore and protect fluid/electrolyte balance and perfusion; establish NPO + NG decompression with aspiration precautions; vigilantly MONITOR for strangulation/perforation and escalate emergently; control pain/nausea; and coordinate diagnostics, surgical consultation, and possible operative care while teaching the patient throughout.

patient.name: Harold P.
patient.code: Full code; goal = stabilize fluid/electrolyte status, decompress the bowel (NPO + NG suction), monitor for strangulation/perforation, and coordinate conservative-vs-surgical management of a mechanical small bowel obstruction
patient.admitDate: A 64-year-old man with a history of an open appendectomy and a prior partial colectomy presents to the ED with 2 days of worsening CRAMPY, colicky abdominal pain, marked abdominal DISTENSION, repeated BILIOUS VOMITING, and an inability to pass stool or flatus (OBSTIPATION). His abdomen is distended and tympanitic with high-pitched, tinkling bowel sounds; he is mildly tachycardic and hypotensive with dry mucous membranes (hypovolemia). Imaging shows dilated small-bowel loops with air-fluid levels consistent with a mechanical SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION, most likely from adhesions. He is being managed with NPO, NG decompression, IV fluid/electrolyte resuscitation, and close monitoring for strangulation while surgery is consulted.
patient.room: Surgical med-surg unit; NPO with NG tube to low intermittent suction; IV fluids/electrolyte replacement; strict intake & output; surgical service consulted; monitoring for signs of strangulation/perforation and response to conservative management

vitals.temp: 37.2°C; distended, tympanitic abdomen with high-pitched/tinkling bowel sounds; crampy colicky pain, repeated BILIOUS vomiting, obstipation (no stool/flatus); dry mucous membranes — classic mechanical SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION with early hypovolemia

37.1°C; labs reflect vomiting + third-spacing: HYPOKALEMIA (K 3.1), metabolic ALKALOSIS (HCO3 30, pH 7.49), elevated BUN/creatinine ratio (prerenal), and hemoconcentration; low urine output — fluid/electrolyte resuscitation is an immediate priority

37.0°C; NPO; NG tube to LOW INTERMITTENT SUCTION draining bilious gastric contents (output measured and replaced); isotonic IV fluids + potassium replacement; semi-Fowler's with aspiration precautions; strict I&O — distension and vomiting easing

38.6°C; WARNING pattern that would signal STRANGULATION/perforation — pain becomes SEVERE and CONSTANT (not colicky), fever, tachycardia, hypotension, a RIGID abdomen with rebound/guarding, and rising LACTATE/WBC; this is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY requiring immediate escalation

37.0°C; with bowel rest + decompression + resuscitation, the partial adhesive obstruction resolves: NG output decreases, abdomen softens/less distended, FLATUS and then stool return, and electrolytes normalize — NG tube can be clamped/removed and diet cautiously advanced per surgery

37.0°C; tolerating a progressive diet without vomiting, normal bowel function, comfortable; educated on adhesion-related recurrence risk and the warning signs to return for (recurrent distension/vomiting/obstipation/severe pain); follow-up arranged

labs.n: PRESENTATION + recognizing mechanical SBO (causes + classic picture)
labs.v: Harold P. is a 64-year-old man with PRIOR abdominal surgery (open appendectomy, partial colectomy) presenting with 2 days of CRAMPY, COLICKY, intermittent abdominal PAIN, marked DISTENSION, repeated BILIOUS VOMITING, and OBSTIPATION (no stool or flatus). His abdomen is distended and tympanitic with HIGH-PITCHED, TINKLING bowel sounds, and he is mildly tachycardic/hypotensive with dry mucous membranes — a classic MECHANICAL SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION. The MOST COMMON cause is post-surgical ADHESIONS (very likely here given his surgical history); other causes include incarcerated HERNIAS, TUMORS, VOLVULUS, intussusception, strictures (Crohn), and gallstone ileus. The CLASSIC tetrad is colicky pain + distension + vomiting + obstipation; PROXIMAL obstructions vomit EARLY and profusely (often bilious), while DISTAL obstructions vomit LATER with more distension and FECULENT emesis. Bowel sounds are hyperactive/tinkling EARLY (above the blockage) and become HYPOACTIVE/ABSENT later as the bowel fatigues or ischemia develops. Recognizing this picture — and the patient's adhesion risk — drives the nurse's priorities: stabilize fluids/electrolytes, decompress (NPO + NG), monitor for strangulation, and coordinate management.
labs.r: Mechanical SBO = blockage of intestinal flow; ADHESIONS (prior surgery) are #1 cause (also hernia/tumor/volvulus). Classic: CRAMPY colicky pain + DISTENSION + VOMITING (proximal = early/bilious; distal = late/feculent) + OBSTIPATION (no stool/flatus). Bowel sounds high-pitched/TINKLING early → hypoactive/absent late. RN role: recognize the SBO picture + likely cause.

labs.n: Pathophysiology + types (mechanical vs ileus; partial vs complete; simple vs STRANGULATED) + fluid/electrolyte loss
labs.v: MECHANICAL obstruction (a physical blockage — adhesions, hernia, tumor) must be distinguished from PARALYTIC ILEUS, a FUNCTIONAL failure of peristalsis (post-op, opioids, HYPOKALEMIA, peritonitis, immobility) that produces a silent, distended abdomen with ABSENT bowel sounds and no mechanical lesion. Obstructions are further classified as PARTIAL (some flow continues — often resolves conservatively) vs COMPLETE (no passage of stool/flatus), and — most importantly — SIMPLE vs STRANGULATED/CLOSED-LOOP. A STRANGULATED obstruction has COMPROMISED BLOOD SUPPLY: the bowel becomes ISCHEMIC, then NECROTIC, then PERFORATES, causing peritonitis and sepsis — a SURGICAL EMERGENCY. FLUID & ELECTROLYTE derangement is a central, immediate threat: VOMITING and THIRD-SPACING (fluid sequestered into the distended, edematous bowel lumen and wall) cause HYPOVOLEMIA (tachycardia, hypotension, oliguria, elevated BUN/creatinine ratio, hemoconcentration); loss of potassium-rich gastric/intestinal fluid causes HYPOKALEMIA (which itself can worsen ileus and cause arrhythmias), and loss of gastric acid (proximal/NG losses) causes a metabolic ALKALOSIS, whereas advanced ischemia/sepsis causes a metabolic ACIDOSIS with rising lactate. The nurse anticipates and corrects these derangements while watching for the shift from a simple to a strangulated obstruction.
labs.r: MECHANICAL (physical block) vs PARALYTIC ILEUS (functional — no peristalsis, absent sounds, from post-op/opioids/HYPOKALEMIA/peritonitis). PARTIAL (may resolve) vs COMPLETE; SIMPLE vs STRANGULATED/closed-loop (compromised blood supply → ischemia→necrosis→PERFORATION = surgical emergency). Fluid/lyte: vomiting + THIRD-SPACING → HYPOVOLEMIA + HYPOKALEMIA + metabolic ALKALOSIS (acidosis/↑lactate if ischemic). RN role: anticipate + correct derangements; watch for strangulation.

labs.n: Diagnostics + MONITORING for strangulation/perforation (the surgical-emergency triggers)
labs.v: DIAGNOSIS rests on imaging and serial assessment. An upright/flat abdominal X-RAY shows dilated small-bowel loops with AIR-FLUID LEVELS and a 'STRING-OF-PEARLS' sign, paucity of colonic gas in complete obstruction, and — critically — FREE AIR under the diaphragm if PERFORATION has occurred. A CT scan localizes the obstruction and its TRANSITION POINT, identifies the cause (adhesion, hernia, mass), and detects signs of ISCHEMIA or a CLOSED-LOOP obstruction (bowel-wall thickening, pneumatosis, reduced enhancement). LABS support monitoring: a rising WBC, a rising LACTATE, and a metabolic ACIDOSIS suggest ISCHEMIA/strangulation; electrolytes and renal indices track the fluid deficit. The MOST IMPORTANT ongoing nursing job is to MONITOR for the conversion of a simple obstruction into a STRANGULATED one or a PERFORATION — performing SERIAL abdominal exams and vital signs and watching for these RED FLAGS: pain that becomes SEVERE and CONSTANT rather than intermittent/colicky; FEVER; TACHYCARDIA and hypotension; PERITONEAL SIGNS (rigidity, involuntary guarding, REBOUND tenderness, a board-like abdomen); markedly decreased/absent bowel sounds with worsening distension; and rising lactate/WBC. ANY of these mandates IMMEDIATE escalation to the surgeon — strangulation/perforation is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY where delay costs bowel and life.
labs.r: Imaging: abdominal X-RAY = dilated loops + AIR-FLUID LEVELS + string-of-pearls; FREE AIR = perforation; CT localizes + shows ischemia/closed-loop. Labs: rising WBC/LACTATE + metabolic ACIDOSIS = ischemia. MONITOR for STRANGULATION/perforation: SEVERE CONSTANT pain, fever, tachycardia/hypotension, PERITONEAL signs (rigidity/rebound/guarding), worsening distension → IMMEDIATE surgical escalation. RN role: serial exams/vitals + recognize + escalate the surgical emergency.

labs.n: Management — conservative (NPO/NG/IV) vs surgical; NG care; what to AVOID
labs.v: INITIAL management is supportive and is the SAME first set of steps for nearly every SBO — 'drip and suck' plus resuscitation: (1) NPO (nothing by mouth) to rest the bowel; (2) insert a NASOGASTRIC (NG) TUBE to LOW INTERMITTENT SUCTION to DECOMPRESS the stomach and proximal bowel — this relieves DISTENSION and VOMITING, rests the bowel, and reduces ASPIRATION risk (verify placement, keep patent, irrigate per policy, measure output and replace volume, and provide oral/nares care and aspiration precautions with the head of bed elevated); (3) IV ISOTONIC FLUID resuscitation with CORRECTION of electrolytes — especially POTASSIUM replacement and addressing the alkalosis — guided by STRICT intake/output (urine + NG + emesis) and labs; (4) ANALGESIA and ANTIEMETICS for comfort; (5) SERIAL monitoring. DEFINITIVE management depends on type: a PARTIAL or adhesive obstruction frequently RESOLVES with this conservative care over hours to days (return of flatus/stool, decreasing NG output, softening abdomen); a COMPLETE, CLOSED-LOOP, or STRANGULATED obstruction — or one that fails to resolve or shows ISCHEMIA — requires SURGERY (adhesiolysis, hernia repair, resection of nonviable bowel). The nurse prepares the patient for possible OR (consent, pre-op labs/teaching, NPO already in place). KEY 'DO-NOTs': do NOT give ORAL FEEDING or BULK-FORMING FIBER/oral LAXATIVES to try to 'push the obstruction through' (this worsens distension, vomiting, and aspiration risk); and do NOT give ANTIMOTILITY agents such as LOPERAMIDE to stop the vomiting (slowing the gut worsens an obstruction and risks perforation/toxic dilation).
labs.r: INITIAL (same for most SBO) = 'drip and suck' + resuscitate: NPO; NG tube to LOW INTERMITTENT SUCTION (decompress → relieve distension/vomiting + reduce aspiration; verify placement, measure/replace output, aspiration precautions); IV isotonic FLUIDS + correct electrolytes (replace K, treat alkalosis) with strict I&O; analgesia/antiemetics; serial monitoring. PARTIAL/adhesive often RESOLVES conservatively; COMPLETE/closed-loop/STRANGULATED → SURGERY (adhesiolysis/resection) — prep for OR. AVOID oral feeding/bulk laxatives + AVOID antimotility (loperamide). RN role: decompress + resuscitate + prep for surgery if needed.

labs.n: Nursing care + recovery + discharge teaching; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: ONGOING NURSING CARE: maintain NG patency and accurate measurement/replacement of output; protect the airway (semi-Fowler's, aspiration precautions, oral/nares care); titrate IV fluids/electrolytes to I&O, labs, and hemodynamics; assess pain and the ABDOMEN serially (distension, tenderness, bowel sounds, return of flatus) and watch for the strangulation red flags; provide analgesia/antiemetics; VTE prophylaxis and early mobilization as able; mouth care for the NPO patient; and emotional support and explanation throughout. RECOVERY: improvement is signaled by decreasing NG output, a softening/less-distended abdomen, RETURN OF FLATUS then stool, and normalized electrolytes — the NG tube is then clamped/removed and diet is advanced cautiously (clear liquids → as tolerated) per surgery, watching for recurrence. If surgery was required, post-op care follows (incision/ostomy if created, ileus prevention, ambulation, pain control). DISCHARGE TEACHING: most adhesive obstructions are managed successfully, but adhesions create a lifelong RECURRENCE risk — teach the patient to RETURN for recurrent crampy pain, distension, vomiting, or inability to pass stool/flatus, and to seek emergency care for severe constant pain or fever. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the patient was NOT given oral feeding or bulk laxatives to 'push it through'; was NOT given antimotility agents (loperamide) for the vomiting; the NG tube was NOT clamped or the IV fluids stopped prematurely just because he 'felt better'; and new fever/tachycardia/rigid abdomen were NOT dismissed as constipation. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: early recognition and decompression; prompt fluid/electrolyte correction; vigilant monitoring for strangulation with timely surgical escalation; and a coordinated surgical/medical plan. EXPECTED outcome: a partial adhesive obstruction resolves with conservative management (or is corrected surgically), bowel function returns, fluid/electrolyte balance is restored, and the patient is educated on recurrence. NURSING ROLE: recognize SBO and its cause; stabilize and protect fluids/electrolytes/airway; decompress (NPO + NG suction); monitor for and escalate strangulation/perforation; control pain/nausea; and coordinate diagnostics, surgery, and patient teaching.
labs.r: ONGOING: NG patency + measure/replace output; aspiration precautions/semi-Fowler's/oral care; titrate fluids/electrolytes to I&O+labs; SERIAL abdominal exams + watch strangulation red flags; analgesia/antiemetics; VTE prophylaxis. RECOVERY = ↓NG output + softening abdomen + RETURN OF FLATUS/stool + normalized lytes → clamp/remove NG + advance diet cautiously. TEACH adhesion-recurrence warning signs. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no oral feeding/bulk laxatives, no antimotility, no premature NG clamp/fluid stop, don't dismiss strangulation signs. RN role: decompress + resuscitate + monitor/escalate + coordinate + teach.

nursesNote: Surgical med-surg unit — Harold P., 64, with PRIOR abdominal surgery (open appendectomy + partial colectomy). 2 days of CRAMPY colicky abdominal pain, marked DISTENSION, repeated BILIOUS VOMITING, OBSTIPATION (no stool/flatus). Abdomen distended/tympanitic with HIGH-PITCHED/TINKLING bowel sounds; mildly tachycardic/hypotensive, dry mucous membranes (hypovolemia). Imaging: dilated small-bowel loops + AIR-FLUID LEVELS → mechanical SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION, likely ADHESIONS (#1 cause; also hernia/tumor/volvulus). Type matters: mechanical vs paralytic ileus (functional — absent sounds, post-op/opioids/HYPOKALEMIA/peritonitis); partial (may resolve) vs complete; SIMPLE vs STRANGULATED/closed-loop (compromised blood supply → ischemia→necrosis→PERFORATION = surgical emergency). Fluid/lyte: vomiting + THIRD-SPACING → hypovolemia + HYPOKALEMIA (K 3.1) + metabolic ALKALOSIS (HCO3 30/pH 7.49) + prerenal azotemia + low UOP. MANAGEMENT ('drip and suck' + resuscitate): NPO; NG tube to LOW INTERMITTENT SUCTION (decompress → relieve distension/vomiting + reduce aspiration; verify placement, keep patent, MEASURE + REPLACE output, oral/nares care); IV isotonic FLUIDS + POTASSIUM replacement + treat alkalosis, titrated to STRICT I&O (urine+NG+emesis) + labs; semi-Fowler's + aspiration precautions; analgesia + antiemetics; VTE prophylaxis. MONITOR for STRANGULATION/perforation (serial abdominal exams + vitals): RED FLAGS = SEVERE CONSTANT pain (not colicky), FEVER, tachycardia, hypotension, PERITONEAL signs (rigidity/rebound/guarding), worsening distension, rising LACTATE/WBC, metabolic acidosis → IMMEDIATE surgical escalation (free air on X-ray = perforation). DEFINITIVE: partial/adhesive often RESOLVES conservatively (↓NG output, softening abdomen, RETURN OF FLATUS/stool, normalized lytes → clamp/remove NG + advance diet cautiously); COMPLETE/closed-loop/STRANGULATED or failure-to-resolve → SURGERY (adhesiolysis/hernia repair/resection) — prep for OR (consent, pre-op). AVOID: oral feeding or bulk-forming fiber/oral laxatives to 'push it through' (worsens distension/vomiting/aspiration); ANTIMOTILITY agents (loperamide) for vomiting (worsen obstruction, risk perforation/toxic dilation). DISCHARGE TEACHING: adhesions = lifelong recurrence risk → return for recurrent distension/vomiting/obstipation/severe pain or fever. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT feed orally/give bulk laxatives, did NOT give antimotility, did NOT clamp NG/stop fluids prematurely on 'feels better,' did NOT dismiss strangulation signs as constipation. Coordinated surgical/medical plan; patient updated throughout.
providerOrders: ADMIT: surgical med-surg unit; mechanical small bowel obstruction (likely adhesive); surgery consulted
DIAGNOSIS: small bowel obstruction — monitor for progression to strangulation/perforation
CODE STATUS: full code
DIET/GI: NPO; nasogastric tube to LOW INTERMITTENT suction; verify placement, keep patent (irrigate per policy), measure output each shift and replace volume per orders
FLUIDS/ELECTROLYTES:
IV isotonic crystalloid resuscitation; replace potassium per protocol; correct metabolic alkalosis
Strict intake & output (urine, NG, emesis); daily weights; titrate fluids to I&O, labs, and hemodynamics
MONITORING:
Serial abdominal assessments (distension, tenderness, bowel sounds, passage of flatus/stool) and vital signs

Notify surgeon IMMEDIATELY for strangulation/perforation signs: severe constant pain, fever, tachycardia/hypotension, peritoneal signs (rigidity/rebound/guarding), rising lactate/WBC, or free air on imaging
DIAGNOSTICS:
Abdominal X-ray (upright/flat) and CT abdomen/pelvis as ordered; CBC, CMP, lactate, type & screen
COMFORT/SAFETY:
Analgesia and antiemetics per orders; semi-Fowler's position with aspiration precautions; frequent oral and nares care
VTE prophylaxis; early mobilization as tolerated
AVOID:
No oral feeding or bulk-forming fiber/oral laxatives to 'push through' the obstruction
No antimotility agents (e.g., loperamide) for the vomiting
SURGERY:

Prepare for possible operative management (lysis of adhesions/hernia repair/resection) if complete, closed-loop, strangulated, or failing conservative management — consent, pre-op labs, NPO maintained
DISCHARGE TEACHING (when resolved):
Adhesion-related recurrence risk; return for recurrent crampy pain, distension, vomiting, or inability to pass stool/flatus; emergency care for severe constant pain or fever; surgical follow-up

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this patient with a likely bowel obstruction?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Crampy, colicky abdominal pain with distension, bilious vomiting, and obstipation (no stool or flatus) — the classic mechanical SBO picture
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the classic SBO tetrad. RN role: recognize mechanical obstruction.

opts.text: Bowel sounds high-pitched and tinkling early (above the obstruction), becoming hypoactive/absent later
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the characteristic bowel-sound evolution of mechanical obstruction. RN role: assess and interpret bowel sounds.

opts.text: History of prior abdominal surgery — adhesions are the most common cause of small bowel obstruction
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — surgical history points to the likely adhesive cause. RN role: identify the probable etiology.

opts.text: Hypovolemia and electrolyte loss — tachycardia, hypotension, dry mucous membranes, low urine output, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis from vomiting and third-spacing
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fluid/electrolyte derangement is the immediate physiologic threat. RN role: recognize and correct hypovolemia/electrolytes.

opts.text: Watch for STRANGULATION — severe constant (not colicky) pain, fever, tachycardia, a rigid/rebound abdomen, and rising lactate/WBC indicate ischemia/perforation and a surgical emergency
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing the simple-to-strangulated conversion is critical and time-sensitive. RN role: monitor for and escalate strangulation.

opts.text: NPO with NG tube decompression to low intermittent suction, plus IV fluids — and imaging to confirm the obstruction (dilated loops/air-fluid levels; free air = perforation)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — decompression/resuscitation and confirmatory imaging are the foundational steps. RN role: decompress, resuscitate, and obtain diagnostics.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine before medications/surgery. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Give the patient a full oral meal and bulk-forming fiber/oral laxatives to 'push the blockage through'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The patient must be NPO; oral feeding and bulking/laxatives worsen distension, vomiting, and aspiration risk and do not relieve a mechanical obstruction.

opts.text: Administer loperamide (an antimotility agent) to stop the vomiting and 'rest the bowel'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Antimotility agents slow the gut, worsen an obstruction, and risk perforation/toxic dilation; they are contraindicated. Decompress with an NG tube instead.

opts.text: A mechanical SBO is a physical blockage of intestinal flow; adhesions from prior surgery are the most common cause, followed by hernias, tumors, and volvulus
opts.rat: CORRECT — definition + etiology. Foundation for recognition.

opts.text: A mechanical obstruction (hyperactive/tinkling then absent sounds, distension) differs from a paralytic ileus — a functional loss of peristalsis (post-op, opioids, hypokalemia, peritonitis) with a silent, distended abdomen and no mechanical lesion
opts.rat: CORRECT — mechanical vs functional distinction. Foundation for correct management.

opts.text: Vomiting and third-spacing cause hypovolemia, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis, so fluid/electrolyte correction is an early priority
opts.rat: CORRECT — fluid/electrolyte consequences. Foundation for resuscitation.

opts.text: A strangulated or closed-loop obstruction has compromised blood supply leading to ischemia, necrosis, and perforation — a surgical emergency
opts.rat: CORRECT — the critical strangulation concept. Foundation for monitoring/escalation.

opts.text: Partial/adhesive obstructions often resolve with conservative management (NPO, NG decompression, IV fluids), while complete, closed-loop, or strangulated obstructions require surgery
opts.rat: CORRECT — conservative-vs-surgical principle. Foundation for the plan of care.

opts.text: NG tube decompression to low intermittent suction relieves distension and vomiting, rests the bowel, and reduces aspiration risk
opts.rat: CORRECT — rationale for NG decompression. Foundation for the key intervention.

opts.text: The priority treatment for every bowel obstruction is immediate oral feeding to stimulate motility
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The patient is kept NPO; oral feeding worsens distension/vomiting/aspiration and does not relieve a mechanical obstruction.

opts.text: A strangulated obstruction is a minor issue that can safely be observed for several days without surgery
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Strangulation is a surgical emergency — delay leads to necrosis, perforation, peritonitis, and sepsis; it requires prompt operative intervention.

opts.text: Establish NPO and NG tube decompression to low intermittent suction with aspiration precautions (relieve distension/vomiting, protect the airway, rest the bowel)
opts.rat: #1 — Foundational + protects the airway from vomiting/aspiration while decompressing. RN role: NPO + NG decompression.

opts.text: Restore fluid/electrolyte balance and perfusion — IV isotonic fluids, potassium replacement, correct alkalosis, strict I&O
opts.rat: #2 — High. Hypovolemia and hypokalemia are immediate physiologic threats. RN role: fluid/electrolyte resuscitation.

opts.text: Monitor vigilantly for strangulation/perforation — serial abdominal exams, vitals, lactate/WBC — and escalate emergently
opts.rat: #3 — Critical and ongoing. Detecting the simple-to-strangulated conversion drives emergency surgery. RN role: monitor + escalate.

opts.text: Control pain and nausea and maintain comfort/positioning
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Analgesia/antiemetics improve comfort once stabilization and monitoring are underway. RN role: pain/nausea management.

opts.text: Coordinate diagnostics and surgical consultation and prepare for possible operative management
opts.rat: #5 — Important + ongoing. Imaging, surgical consult, and OR prep define definitive care. RN role: coordinate diagnostics + surgery.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient with an SBO.

opts.text: Keep the patient NPO and insert an NG tube to low intermittent suction; verify placement, keep it patent, and measure/record output
opts.rat: Core decompression and bowel rest. RN role: NPO + NG suction.

opts.text: Start IV isotonic fluids, replace potassium per orders, correct the alkalosis, and monitor electrolytes with strict intake & output
opts.rat: Corrects the immediate hypovolemia/electrolyte threat. RN role: fluid/electrolyte resuscitation + I&O.

opts.text: Position in semi-Fowler's, maintain aspiration precautions, and provide frequent oral/nares care
opts.rat: Protects the airway in a vomiting/NG patient and maintains comfort. RN role: aspiration precautions + comfort.

opts.text: Perform serial abdominal assessments and monitor vitals/lactate/WBC; escalate emergently if severe constant pain, fever, or peritoneal signs develop
opts.rat: Detects and escalates strangulation/perforation. RN role: serial monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: Obtain ordered imaging (abdominal X-ray/CT) and labs and consult surgery; prepare for possible operative management
opts.rat: Confirms diagnosis/type and arranges definitive care. RN role: diagnostics + surgical coordination.

opts.text: Provide ordered analgesia and antiemetics and reassess the response
opts.rat: Appropriate comfort management with reassessment. RN role: pain/nausea control.

opts.text: Offer a full oral diet and bulk-forming fiber/oral laxatives to push the obstruction through
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the patient must be NPO; oral feeding/bulking worsens distension, vomiting, and aspiration risk and does not relieve a mechanical obstruction.

opts.text: Give loperamide (an antimotility agent) to stop the vomiting
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — antimotility agents worsen an obstruction and risk perforation/toxic dilation; decompress with the NG tube instead.

opts.text: Clamp the NG tube and discontinue IV fluids because the patient 'feels better' after initial decompression
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — premature clamping/stopping fluids before resolution risks recurrent distension/vomiting and rebound hypovolemia; continue per the plan and surgery's guidance.

opts.text: Encourage ambulation with a heating pad and dismiss new fever, tachycardia, and a rigid abdomen as ordinary constipation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — those are strangulation/perforation red flags requiring immediate surgical escalation, not reassurance; missing them risks necrosis and sepsis.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Establish NPO, insert the NG tube to low intermittent suction, and position with aspiration precautions
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Decompression relieves distension/vomiting and protects the airway while resting the bowel. RN role: NPO + NG decompression.

opts.text: Start IV fluid resuscitation and replace electrolytes (potassium) per orders; monitor strict intake & output
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Correcting hypovolemia and hypokalemia addresses the immediate physiologic threat. RN role: fluid/electrolyte resuscitation.

opts.text: Obtain ordered imaging and labs and notify/consult surgery to confirm the type and plan
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Diagnostics and surgical input establish whether conservative or operative management is needed. RN role: diagnostics + consult.

opts.text: Continuously monitor for strangulation/perforation (serial abdominal exams, vitals, lactate/WBC) and escalate emergently if it develops
opts.rat: #4 — Ongoing/critical. Vigilant surveillance catches the surgical emergency early. RN role: monitor + escalate.

opts.text: Provide analgesia/antiemetics, comfort, and prepare the patient for possible surgery (consent, pre-op teaching), reassessing the response
opts.rat: #5 — Comfort + readiness. Pain/nausea control and OR preparation round out care once stabilization and monitoring are in place. RN role: comfort + surgical prep.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: After NG decompression and IV fluids, distension and vomiting decrease, NG output declines, and flatus then stool return
opts.rat: Decompression + bowel rest resolving the obstruction. Expected, desired response.

opts.text: Electrolytes and urine output normalize with isotonic fluids and potassium replacement, and the partial adhesive obstruction resolves with conservative management
opts.rat: Successful fluid/electrolyte correction + conservative resolution. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: After surgical lysis of a complete/strangulated obstruction, the patient recovers, bowel function returns, and diet is advanced gradually
opts.rat: Appropriate operative management of a surgical obstruction with recovery. Improving.

opts.text: The NG tube is patent and draining, the abdomen is softening and less tender, and pain is decreasing — conservative management is working
opts.rat: Objective signs that bowel rest/decompression are succeeding. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — staff gave a full oral diet and bulk laxatives; vomiting worsened, the patient aspirated, and distension increased
opts.rat: ⚠️ Oral feeding/bulking a mechanical obstruction worsens distension/vomiting and caused aspiration. Demonstrates why the patient stays NPO with NG decompression.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — loperamide was given to stop the vomiting; the obstruction worsened with increasing distension and pain
opts.rat: ⚠️ Antimotility agents worsen an obstruction and risk perforation. Demonstrates why they are contraindicated.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — new fever, tachycardia, severe constant pain, a rigid/rebound abdomen, and rising lactate/WBC were dismissed as constipation; the bowel strangulated and perforated
opts.rat: ⚠️ Missed strangulation/perforation — the dismissed red flags are a surgical emergency. Demonstrates the need for serial monitoring and immediate escalation.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the NG tube was clamped and IV fluids stopped early because he 'felt better'; distension and vomiting recurred and he became hypovolemic again
opts.rat: ⚠️ Premature discontinuation before true resolution caused rebound obstruction/hypovolemia. Demonstrates continuing decompression/resuscitation until resolved per surgery.

opts.text: Early in management, before decompression and fluids take full effect, distension and pain are present and essentially unchanged while the nurse continues NG suction and resuscitation
opts.rat: Before bowel rest/resuscitation take effect, an unchanged baseline is expected; continue decompression, fluids, and serial monitoring and reassess.

opts.text: At follow-up the patient tolerates a regular diet, has normal bowel function, and verbalizes the warning signs of recurrence and adhesion-related risk
opts.rat: Sustained recovery with effective discharge teaching. Ideal longer-term outcome.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
